opp.name: Renuity_S2P+Intake_June'26
opp.next_step: 6/19/26 MQ Alec notified me that we are one of the vendors qualified to participate in the RFP phase. RFP is slated to be issued by 7/10. 6/12/23 MQ RFI submitted. Qualified vendors will be notified of their inclusion in RFP phase. 6/3/26. MQ Initial call completed. Opportunity is Qualified. Renuity currently relies on manual processes across all procurement functions. They are a PE backed company that has recently acquired a variety of residential services based companies that span across the US. Procurement is decentralized. Alec has been tasked by COO to find an S2P solution that meets their requirements. Alec is inviting Zycus to participate in their RFI. Submissions are due 6/12. Based on responses, Renuity will select 3 vendors to participate in susequent RFP planned to be released 6/26.
opp.ais_why:
• Renuity's Alec Diaz is 'building the indirect procurement function from the ground up,' aiming to centralize decentralized P-card and manual processes ‚Äî foundational modernization, not an AI mandate.
• Agentic AI, Merlin and Merlin Intake are scoped and the deal is at Formal Evaluation via an RFI-to-RFP path, so AI could feature in selection.
• No agentic-platform discussion or AI sentiment is recorded yet, keeping appetite in the curious range.

items.quote: the ROI is gonna be paying for the system within a year or two

items.area: Named competitors in the RFI/RFP shortlist
items.quote: obviously, looking at other solutions as well

items.area: Buyer-side contact roles on the opportunity (0 logged)

sources: Avoma intro call 3 Jun 2026 (Alec Diaz, Marc Quessenberry)
metrics.narrative: Alec Diaz framed the value qualitatively but no dollar figure has been quantified yet. On the intro call (3 Jun 2026) he described indirect spend as fully decentralized, 'everything's on p-cards, on personal cards, where we get random invoices sent into our e-AP email, that's manually processed', with no national account for indirect spend, which is the cost-leakage and Maverick-spend problem to be sized. Marc Quessenberry tied ROI directly to consolidating that Maverick spend ('it makes my ROI much easier... the ROI is gonna be paying for the system within a year or two'), but neither party put numbers to current spend, savings target, or headcount cost. Quantifying the addressable indirect spend and a target savings percentage is the move, and it is exactly what the buyer needs to build the business case he must present to the ELT.

champion.narrative: Alec Diaz is a developing champion: he is the senior manager of indirect procurement, owns the evaluation, invited Zycus into the RFI, was transparent that he is 'looking at other solutions as well', and was visibly engaged on the 3 Jun 2026 call (asking detailed product questions on Teams intake, punch-outs, and approval workflows). What has developed is access to the process and genuine product interest; what is still missing is proof of internal advocacy for Zycus specifically and any line to the COO/ELT. He reports to the COO but does not control budget, so he is a coach/buyer-side champion who needs arming with the business case and an exec introduction to become decisive.

sources: Avoma intro call 3 Jun 2026 (Alec Diaz, Marc Quessenberry)
competition.narrative: This is an openly competitive multi-vendor RFI/RFP. Alec Diaz said directly on the 3 Jun 2026 call: 'to be transparent, obviously, looking at other solutions as well', and the buyer plans to shortlist 3 vendors for the RFP. No specific competitor was named in the evidence read this sweep, so the rival set remains unnamed. Zycus's differentiation wedge from the call is the single-code-base, AI-native suite (Merlin intake/orchestration in Teams, Anna autonomous negotiation, AP Smartdesk, iRisk) versus competitors who 'go out and buy a solution... then try to integrate that' and 'have nightmares with the integration piece.' Winning requires landing that AI/ease-of-use story hard in the RFP and demo; the move is to name the other shortlisted vendors so positioning can be sharpened.

identify_pain.narrative: The operational pain is concrete and owned by Alec Diaz, who is the sole person standing up indirect procurement at a PE-backed roll-up that has grown 'tremendously over the last 6 to 7 years through our acquisitions.' He stated procurement is 'very decentralized. Everything's on p-cards, on personal cards, where we get random invoices sent into our e-AP email, that's manually processed', with 'really no national account for indirect spend' and no controls over branch-level Maverick spend. He is tasked with consolidating all indirect suppliers and building the function 'from the ground up.' This is a clear, confirmed pain with a named owner; the gap is quantification (see Metrics), not whether the pain exists.

paper_process.narrative: No contracting mechanics have been discussed yet, which is appropriate this early in Formal Evaluation. There is no signal on MSA/SOW, legal review, procurement channel, or InfoSec, and no SI/partner involvement named. Renuity runs Acumatica as its ERP (stated by Alec, 3 Jun 2026), so an integration/InfoSec review will eventually be in scope. The move is to surface the contracting and security path during the RFP/demo phase so it does not compress the timeline near the 31 Oct close.

economic_buyer.narrative: The economic decision sits above Alec Diaz with the COO and the ELT, and Alec described the approval path clearly on the 3 Jun 2026 call: the COO tasked him to 'do an RFP... vet it down into one vendor and kinda come up with the business case and ROI, present it out to the ELT, and then get the buy-in and approval from them.' So the budget/approval authority is identified by role (COO sponsor, ELT approver) but neither the COO nor any ELT member is named, has joined a call, or is engaged. Alec runs the evaluation but does not control the budget. The move is to get the COO named and into the room before the business case lands, since the deal is won or lost on ELT buy-in he does not yet own.

sources: SF Next Step notes 3 Jun / 12 Jun / 19 Jun 2026
decision_process.narrative: The buyer's process is explicit and well-mapped from the 3 Jun 2026 call and the SF Next Step trail: RFI issued (responses due ~12 Jun) → shortlist 3 vendors → issue RFP the week after 4 July (Next Step updates this to slated for 10 Jul, confirmed 19 Jun) → demos with a prior discovery/solution-consultant session → vet to a single vendor → build business case and ROI → present to the ELT for buy-in and approval → contracting. Zycus has cleared the first gate, confirmed 19 Jun 2026 as a qualified RFP vendor. The chief unknown is the internal ELT approval mechanics (who, how many, what bar), which sits behind Alec.

decision_criteria.narrative: Criteria are emerging from a single discovery call and an RFI Alec drafted, not yet a formal scored list. Alec named his target functional scope directly: 'eProcurement, spend analysis, prior management, and eSourcing... and then also AI capabilities as well', and said this 'is kind of everything that I'm really looking for in a tool.' He probed specifically on Microsoft Teams-based conversational procurement and on punch-out catalogs pulling in additional vendors, signaling intake/orchestration and ease-of-use matter to him. He also confirmed Gartner positioning influenced his interest ('I saw you guys on Gartner'). The full weighted criteria will surface in the RFP document expected ~10 Jul; obtaining and mapping to that RFP is the criteria-confirmation move.

cro_panel.intro: One read per score, in language a CRO/CO can act on. We're behind on this one — the reads below say why and what would change it.

Buyer reply received 19 Jun and last activity 23 Jun (7 days ago) confirm a live, engaged deal; remaining milestones (RFP response, demo, vendor selection, business case, ELT approval, contracting) fit comfortably inside 123 days if cadence holds.

competitive_position.summary: Competitive multi-vendor RFI/RFP with 3 vendors to be shortlisted; the other rivals are not yet named in any evidence. The current single threat is being out-positioned on AI/ease-of-use or price during the RFP and demo. Zycus's wedge is its single-code-base, AI-native suite (Merlin intake in Teams, Anna negotiation agent, AP Smartdesk, iRisk) versus competitors who bolt on acquired modules.
